		Parcel Number:15 181 05 002 *Block:--Lot:--Subdivision:--

The subject tree is an exceptionally large, mature tree estimated from photographic evidence to be approximately 65–85 feet in height, with an estimated trunk diameter of approximately 36–48 inches and a canopy spread potentially exceeding 55 feet. The tree contains multiple large structural limbs and upright leaders, several of which extend over or toward occupied portions of the property and nearby structures. The trunk is positioned in immediate proximity to a residential backyard area.The size and location of the tree create a high-consequence target zone should a major limb, leader, or portion of the tree fail. Additional concerns include a visible area of apparent trunk damage or cavity formation shown in the photographic documentation, substantial overhead limb weight, and uneven conditions within the root zone. Because photographs cannot establish internal decay, root stability, or likelihood of failure, an urgent Level 2 or Level 3 tree risk assessment by a qualified arborist is requested. The assessment should specifically examine the trunk wound or cavity, major branch unions, co-dominant or multiple leader attachments, internal decay potential, root plate stability, canopy weight distribution, and the likelihood and consequences of branch or whole-tree failure.The size and location of the tree create a high-consequence target zone should a major limb, leader, or portion of the tree fail. Additional concerns include a visible area of apparent trunk damage or cavity formation shown in the photographic documentation, substantial overhead limb weight, and uneven conditions within the root zone. Because photographs cannot establish internal decay, root stability, or likelihood of failure, an urgent Level 2 or Level 3 tree risk assessment by a qualified arborist is requested. The assessment should specifically examine the trunk wound or cavity, major branch unions, co-dominant or multiple leader attachments, internal decay potential, root plate stability, canopy weight distribution, and the likelihood and consequences of branch or whole-tree failure.Reason for Removal:Invasive/undesirable treeComments:The size and location of the tree create a high-consequence danger zone or fall hazard should a major limb, leader, or portion of the tree fail. The trunk is positioned in immediate proximity to a residential backyard area regularly used by an 80-year-old resident.

		Parcel Number:17 01540008036 *Block:--Lot:--Subdivision:--

The subject Sweetgum is located in the back center of the property and exhibits a significant pocket of decay on the home-facing side of the main trunk. The tree is codominant, with one primary stem exhibiting a pronounced lean toward the residence. Visible decay extends approximately halfway up the main trunk, indicating a high likelihood of significant internal structural deterioration. Due to the extent and location of the decay, selective removal of only one codominant stem is not considered a feasible mitigation option. In the event of partial or total tree failure, there is a high potential for severe damage to the residence and surrounding improvements. Based on the observed structural defects and elevated target exposure, removal of the tree is recommended to mitigate the risk of failure.Reason for Removal:Dying tree
		
		
			Tree number:2Species:Multi-Stem River BirchTree Size (DBH):6Tree location: Right Side Property LineDescription of Tree:This multi-stem River Birch is located between the subject property and the neighboring property on the right side. Removal is requested to provide the necessary equipment access required to safely perform the removal of the hazardous 40-inch Sweetgum. Due to the size of the Sweetgum and the machinery required for its removal, retaining this River Birch would prevent safe and efficient access to the work area.Reason for Removal:Dying tree
		
		
			Tree number:3Species:River Birch Tree Size (DBH):7Tree location:front rightDescription of Tree:obstructs the required access route to the hazardous Sweetgum in the rear of the property. Removal of this tree is necessary to establish a safe equipment access corridor for the removal of the hazardous Sweetgum and to allow the work to be completed in accordance with ISA Best Management Practices.Reason for Removal:Dying tree
